EPISODE_NARRATIVE: PLEASE NOTE, for hail, wind, heavy rain and flooding, please see separate Storm Data entry for May 20-22. ||Numerous tornadoes occurred on this day with numerous injuries and four fatalities at Greenfield in Adair County.||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall, damaging winds, hail and destructive tornadoes to the state. These thunderstorms were the result of a low pressure system which lifted north into the area on the night of May 20th, 2024, producing slow moving storms that dropped 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding over portions of northern and central Iowa through the morning of May 21st, 2024.||Following the heavy rainfall through the morning, skies cleared out ahead of an approaching cold front. By the afternoon hours of May 21st, 2024, this cold front produced a line of strong thunderstorms which produced multiple strong tornadoes throughout the state, including one which led to significant damage in the town of Greenfield, IA, among other locations. This line of storms also produced damaging winds, leading to widespread tree damage and power outages throughout the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This large and violent tornado entered into south central Adair County from Adams County.  The tornado remained a multi-vortex circulation as it moved into the county producing high end damage for much of its remaining path.  The tornado encountered several houses in the vicinity of Fontanelle Road and 320th Street.  Many of the structures received at least EF2 damage with one house north of the intersection receiving up to EF4 damage with a sufficiently anchored structure destroyed and slumped into a debris field nearby.  Additional EF4 damage was noted at a farmstead on Jordan Avenue south of 280th Street with the house destroyed and trees chewed down to only the largest trucks and portions of the bark removed.  The path then passed through another wind mill area with additional turbines collapsing west of Kent Avenue.  The tornado then passed over Nodaway Park Ponds and entered the southwest side of Greenfield along SW 3rd Street.  Several structures over the next several blocks had up to EF4 damage from SW 3rd Street over the SE 3rd Street and South of Linn Street and north of Oak and Noble Streets.   Most of these saw complete destruction of the house with much of the debris scattered in the area but not completely swept away.  The tornado weakened slightly for the later half of the destructive path through Greenfield.  EF3 damage was widespread from SE Kent Street and SE 4th Street to the Intersection of E Iowa Street and NE 8th Street.  The tornado exited the east side of Greenfield near the Adair County Fairgrounds.  The tornado continued east northeast and gradually weakened impacting a few more houses before dissipating just south of the intersection of 220th Street and Quebec Avenue.  This tornado produced at least 35 injuries in addition to 4 deaths within Greenfield.  All of the deaths occurred in homes.  Of note, according to media reports, a Doppler on Wheels (DOW) intercept research team measured a wind speed of just over 300 mph approximately 150 feet above ground level with this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large tree limb down and powerlines down. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection from a passing upper level trough and associated MCS in the morning brought an initial round of non-severe storms mainly in Minnesota into the far reaches of northern Iowa. However, severe weather potential increased by the afternoon as the aforementioned trough and surface boundary made its way into Iowa. Conditions ahead of the boundary included 40-50 knot values of 0-6 km bulk shear, along with 3000-4000J/kg of instability, 25-30 knots of 0-3 km bulk shear and DCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg. As a result, a line of showers and storms developed in the afternoon and passed over central Iowa into the evening, with a handful of hail reports in central Iowa generally from penny-size to 2 inches, while the greater majority of reports involved severe wind gusts of 55-70+ mph and associated damage. Tornadic activity within the line of storms did occur, with a tornado developing in the Des Moines Metro area, causing primarily significant tree damage, as well as some structural and power-pole damage in the area as well. Heavy rain given plenty of warm and moist air over the area also resulted in isolated flash flooding, specifically over Black Hawk county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed spotter report of quarter-sized hail in Arcadia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of thunderstorms developed across western South Dakota during the evening hours of July 13 and spread eastward through the night. The environment featured 2000-3000 J/kg MUCAPE, 25 to 35 knots of deep-layer shear, and an increasingly warm and dry capped layer further eastward through South Dakota. Widespread damaging wind developed with the mesoscale convective system with gusts from 60 to 75 mph common. The storms weakened rapidly as they entered northwest Iowa, but still managed to cause some damage just over the border from South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several small trees and numerous branches in Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal convective development along a weak surface trough axis produced a brief tornadic spin up.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout developed just west of Redwing Avenue around one-half mile north of 380th Street. The landspout tracked generally northwest over open farmland before it lifted just south of 370th Street. Visible damage was not indicated via satellite or from reconnaissance of the area. The location was triangulated from landmarks on a drone video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few diurnal thunderstorms developed along a surface boundary from southwest Minnesota into northwest Iowa, which had been reinforced by earlier day convection. The strongest storm along the line produced hail up to penny size.

Storms developed along a weak surface boundary within a moisture-rich environment with weak mean-layer flow. Slow moving storms produced rainfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour, with amounts of 3.09 inches reported by a cooperative weather observer near Wakefield and personal automated gauges and radar estimates up to 4 inches over portions of the Sioux City metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streets were flooded around the city with water ranging from curb depth to as high as the tops of tires at numerous locations. The worst flooded locations included Nicollet Street, East 23rd Street, Garretson Avenue, Hennepin Street, and Jackson Street.  Pulaski Park was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of April 26th, a potent negatively-tilted shortwave trough ejected out into the central Plains. Upper air analysis shows a jet streak on the downstream side of this trough with substantial divergence aloft over Nebraska. At the surface, morning cloud cover and drizzle across much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa quickly gave way to partly cloud skies as a warm front moved north through the area, just after noon. At the same time, thunderstorms initiated along a pacific front/dryline in south-central Nebraska. One supercell produced several tornadoes from south-central Nebraska into Boone County, resulting in an EF-2 tornado just west of Cedar Rapids. A second round of supercells initiated in Jefferson and Saline counties. These long-track supercells produced the worst tornado outbreak the Omaha WFO has seen in 10 years. 5 EF-3 tornadoes were surveyed, with several of these tornadoes just shy of an EF-4 rating. One of these EF-3 tornadoes impacted the northeast side of Lincoln, Nebraska where 70 people were reported trapped in a manufacturing plant that collapsed as the tornado passed. Another long-track EF-3 tornado hit portions of Elkhorn, Bennington and Blair, Nebraska. Eppley Airfield was hit by the third EF-3 tornado of the day, doing damage to aircraft hangars on the southeast side of the airport before hitting homes in far western Pottawattamie County. The last two EF-3 tornadoes of the day both impacted portions of Pottawattamie County. While one remained primarily in rural areas in the central part of the county, the final EF-3 of the day hit the town of Minden, Iowa head-on, resulting in 1 fatality and 3 injuries. This was the only fatality of the entire event. This tornado continued into Shelby County, narrowly missing the towns of Tenant and Harlan. A total of 24 tornadoes were confirmed across both Nebraska and Iowa portions of the Omaha CWA. The strongest tornado was the EF-3 that impacted Elkhorn and Blair, with peak estimated wind speeds at 165 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damage path began approximately 1 mile north of Pacific Junction, where the tornado was also reported by a trained spotter. The tornado crossed Highway 34 one mile east of Interstate 29 where it snapped trees. It then moved up and into the bluffs, where a home took a direct hit and lost large portions of the roof. A large camper was flipped on its side and trees were also snapped at this location. The tornado progressed north-northeast through a forested area where many tree trunks were snapped. The end of the damage path appeared to be near Pony Creek Park, just to the east of the Pony Creek Lake dam. Peak winds in this tornado were estimated at 100 miles per hour. The maximum measured width was 80 yards, with an estimated average width of 30 yards for this tornado track.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado damage path began near Aspen Road, just west of 240th Street. The tornado tracked toward the north-northeast, snapping trees and electrical poles as it went. A home was impacted along Chestnut Road with substantial roof damage. Two additional homes were directly impacted when the tornado crossed Highway 92. One of these sustained major roof damage while the other home had most external walls collapse while interior walls remained standing. Numerous trees were snapped and a large shed was completely destroyed at this location while a large steel horse trailer was thrown more than a quarter mile. The tornado continued to Cottonwood Road where it damaged a home and an outbuilding. The most significant damage along the track occurred at Dogwood Road where a home was completely destroyed, and only the basement remained. Tree damage here was substantial as well with numerous snapped trunks. Residents of this home survived by seeking shelter in the basement bathroom, which was one of the few locations in the home not directly impacted by major flying debris. The tornado crossed Elmtree Road and Highway 6, and appears to have been at its widest near Highway 6 where it was approximately 800 yards wide. Another home was damaged near 270th and Hickory Road. The tornado continued northeast, damaging electrical poles, trees, and outbuildings all the way to where it ended near 295th and Juniper Road. The ending location is based on photo evidence of spotters located southeast of the storm. Peak winds in this tornado were estimated at 145 miles per hour. This tornado had a max width of 900 yards with an estimated average width of 500 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection from a passing upper level trough and associated MCS in the morning brought an initial round of non-severe storms mainly in Minnesota into the far reaches of northern Iowa. However, severe weather potential increased by the afternoon as the aforementioned trough and surface boundary made its way into Iowa. Conditions ahead of the boundary included 40-50 knot values of 0-6 km bulk shear, along with 3000-4000J/kg of instability, 25-30 knots of 0-3 km bulk shear and DCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg. As a result, a line of showers and storms developed in the afternoon and passed over central Iowa into the evening, with a handful of hail reports in central Iowa generally from penny-size to 2 inches, while the greater majority of reports involved severe wind gusts of 55-70+ mph and associated damage. Tornadic activity within the line of storms did occur, with a tornado developing in the Des Moines Metro area, causing primarily significant tree damage, as well as some structural and power-pole damage in the area as well. Heavy rain given plenty of warm and moist air over the area also resulted in isolated flash flooding, specifically over Black Hawk county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter submitted a photo of quarter-sized hail 5 miles north of Dysart. Report received was delayed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along a weak surface boundary within a moisture-rich environment with weak mean-layer flow. Slow moving storms produced rainfall rates of 3 to 4 inches per hour, with amounts of 3.09 inches reported by a cooperative weather observer near Wakefield and personal automated gauges and radar estimates up to 4 inches over portions of the Sioux City metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous manhole covers were displaced throughout the city from overwhelmed storm drain systems and the outpouring of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of storms moved across the area on April 26th. The first round was fueled by broad thermal lift and passed across Iowa through the morning with a few of these storms producing small hail but all remained sub severe. The area dried out and cleared out behind this round, allowing for a few hours of destabilization. By afternoon the warm sector was sufficiently unstable between the warm front and a dryline. Robust convection developed along that dryline in the afternoon in western Nebraska, moving into Iowa in the early evening. With sufficient instability, the increasing low level jet, and SRH exceeding 300 m2/s2, large hail and tornadoes occurred with storms across western to central Iowa. In all, 28 tornadoes occurred in Iowa with 18 within the NWS Des Moines area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed within the east side of the Des Moines Metro area just northwest of Easter Lake.  Initial damage reports include damage to trees and fences and then eventually roof damage as the tornado progressed to the northeast.  The tornado passed just to the southeast of James Cownie Soccer Complex and crossed the Des Moines River near the Des Moines Wastewater Treatment Plant.  The tornado then passed into Pleasant Hill with numerous reports of EF2 damage to several houses on the south side of town with widespread roof and wall damage.  The tornado weakened to the northeast and eventually dissipated on the far south side of Altoona. This tornado produced one non-life threatening injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over portions of northern Iowa, especially northwestern Iowa and bordering states later on June 20 through early on June 21. This rainfall caused flash flooding in portions of northwest Iowa and also aided in saturating the soil. As another round of heavy rain fell later on June 21 into the night and morning of June 22, this rainfall led to renewed flash flooding. This rainfall made its way into streams and rivers resulting in significant and record river flooding in northwest Iowa including the West Fork of the Des Moines River. Flooding resulted in massive sandbagging efforts, prolonged road closures and damages, and impacts to home and businesses.||Note: damage estimates are taken from Iowa Homeland Security and Emergency Management Department and represent total flood damages for the entire county for the month of June. Damages are estimated to have surpassed $12 million in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar River at Waterloo crested at 15.69 ft on June 26 at 11:15 am CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain that fell during the month of June across Minnesota and Wisconsin caused rises on the Mississippi River along the Iowa and Illinois border. Additionally, heavy rain that fell locally in July helped to prolong the crest and delay the river falling below flood stage, especially south of the Wapsipinicon, Rock, and Iowa Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River gage at Dubuque Lock and Dam rose above its major flood stage of 20.5 feet around 6 PM CST on July 4 and crested at 20.85 feet on July 6.  The river fell back below major flood stage around 1 AM  CST on July 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded shortwave passed across Iowa in the afternoon, providing a focus for convection as it phased with an increasing southerly moisture stream. Steep lapse rates, plenty of instability, and hodographs supporting rotating updrafts all came together to create an environment supportive of hail producing supercells. Storms remained somewhat discrete and produced swaths of hail across central and southern Iowa. The largest hail reported was up to baseball sized in Appanoose County. A tornado also occurred in Wayne County east of Millerton with the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms lined up across central Iowa on the 850 mb moisture gradient early in the morning of July 31, producing up to quarter sized hail and gusts over 50 mph in isolated downbursts. Storms shifted into southeast Iowa through the morning and remained strong, producing hail and strong winds. As storms shifted southeast the orientation shifted to allow for training storms will heavy rain of 3-6 inches in some locations. This area also received heavier rain the previous day when multiple rounds of storms moved across the area, so soils were less able to handle the additional rain. Areas of flash flooding resulting in road closures were reported. After a break in the afternoon and early evening, a bowing line of storms passed across central to southern Iowa. This brough another round of strong winds of 55 to 65 mph and isolated hail up to quarter sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 3.5 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that a 7 inch diameter tree limb was down. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain that fell during the month of June across Minnesota and Wisconsin caused rises on the Mississippi River along the Iowa and Illinois border. Additionally, heavy rain that fell locally in July helped to prolong the crest and delay the river falling below flood stage, especially south of the Wapsipinicon, Rock, and Iowa Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River at Muscatine rose above its major flood stage of 20 feet around 6 PM on July 4th and crested at 21.48 feet on July 11th. The river fell back below flood stage on July 17th around 7 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection from a passing upper level trough and associated MCS in the morning brought an initial round of non-severe storms mainly in Minnesota into the far reaches of northern Iowa. However, severe weather potential increased by the afternoon as the aforementioned trough and surface boundary made its way into Iowa. Conditions ahead of the boundary included 40-50 knot values of 0-6 km bulk shear, along with 3000-4000J/kg of instability, 25-30 knots of 0-3 km bulk shear and DCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg. As a result, a line of showers and storms developed in the afternoon and passed over central Iowa into the evening, with a handful of hail reports in central Iowa generally from penny-size to 2 inches, while the greater majority of reports involved severe wind gusts of 55-70+ mph and associated damage. Tornadic activity within the line of storms did occur, with a tornado developing in the Des Moines Metro area, causing primarily significant tree damage, as well as some structural and power-pole damage in the area as well. Heavy rain given plenty of warm and moist air over the area also resulted in isolated flash flooding, specifically over Black Hawk county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to multiple hog confinements, with time of occurrence estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over portions of northern Iowa, especially northwestern Iowa and bordering states later on June 20 through early on June 21. This rainfall caused flash flooding in portions of northwest Iowa and also aided in saturating the soil. As another round of heavy rain fell later on June 21 into the night and morning of June 22, this rainfall led to renewed flash flooding. This rainfall made its way into streams and rivers resulting in significant and record river flooding in northwest Iowa including the West Fork of the Des Moines River. Flooding resulted in massive sandbagging efforts, prolonged road closures and damages, and impacts to home and businesses.||Note: damage estimates are taken from Iowa Homeland Security and Emergency Management Department and represent total flood damages for the entire county for the month of June. Damages are estimated to have surpassed $12 million in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines at Stratford crested at 21.56 ft on June 27 at 08:15 pm CDT. The river remained in flood through the end of the month and into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded shortwave passed across Iowa in the afternoon, providing a focus for convection as it phased with an increasing southerly moisture stream. Steep lapse rates, plenty of instability, and hodographs supporting rotating updrafts all came together to create an environment supportive of hail producing supercells. Storms remained somewhat discrete and produced swaths of hail across central and southern Iowa. The largest hail reported was up to baseball sized in Appanoose County. A tornado also occurred in Wayne County east of Millerton with the storms.

Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a pine tree twisted and snapped by thunderstorm winds. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area.  ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch. Flash flooding was reported in Cedar Rapids as well as in parts of Dubuque County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of an 8 inch tree limb down blocking the road. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection from a passing upper level trough and associated MCS in the morning brought an initial round of non-severe storms mainly in Minnesota into the far reaches of northern Iowa. However, severe weather potential increased by the afternoon as the aforementioned trough and surface boundary made its way into Iowa. Conditions ahead of the boundary included 40-50 knot values of 0-6 km bulk shear, along with 3000-4000J/kg of instability, 25-30 knots of 0-3 km bulk shear and DCAPE values around 1000-1500 J/kg. As a result, a line of showers and storms developed in the afternoon and passed over central Iowa into the evening, with a handful of hail reports in central Iowa generally from penny-size to 2 inches, while the greater majority of reports involved severe wind gusts of 55-70+ mph and associated damage. Tornadic activity within the line of storms did occur, with a tornado developing in the Des Moines Metro area, causing primarily significant tree damage, as well as some structural and power-pole damage in the area as well. Heavy rain given plenty of warm and moist air over the area also resulted in isolated flash flooding, specifically over Black Hawk county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail report of 1.25 inches relayed by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over portions of northern Iowa, especially northwestern Iowa and bordering states later on June 20 through early on June 21. This rainfall caused flash flooding in portions of northwest Iowa and also aided in saturating the soil. As another round of heavy rain fell later on June 21 into the night and morning of June 22, this rainfall led to renewed flash flooding. This rainfall made its way into streams and rivers resulting in significant and record river flooding in northwest Iowa including the West Fork of the Des Moines River. Flooding resulted in massive sandbagging efforts, prolonged road closures and damages, and impacts to home and businesses.||Note: damage estimates are taken from Iowa Homeland Security and Emergency Management Department and represent total flood damages for the entire county for the month of June. Damages are estimated to have surpassed $12 million in June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Fork of the Des Moines at Dakota City crested at 21.65 ft on June 24 at 01:30 pm CDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines at Estherville crested at 18.1 ft on June 24 at 09:00 am CDT. The river remained in flood through the end of the month and into July. Preliminary information indicates this is the new flood of record for the point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of April 26th, a potent negatively-tilted shortwave trough ejected out into the central Plains. Upper air analysis shows a jet streak on the downstream side of this trough with substantial divergence aloft over Nebraska. At the surface, morning cloud cover and drizzle across much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa quickly gave way to partly cloud skies as a warm front moved north through the area, just after noon. At the same time, thunderstorms initiated along a pacific front/dryline in south-central Nebraska. One supercell produced several tornadoes from south-central Nebraska into Boone County, resulting in an EF-2 tornado just west of Cedar Rapids. A second round of supercells initiated in Jefferson and Saline counties. These long-track supercells produced the worst tornado outbreak the Omaha WFO has seen in 10 years. 5 EF-3 tornadoes were surveyed, with several of these tornadoes just shy of an EF-4 rating. One of these EF-3 tornadoes impacted the northeast side of Lincoln, Nebraska where 70 people were reported trapped in a manufacturing plant that collapsed as the tornado passed. Another long-track EF-3 tornado hit portions of Elkhorn, Bennington and Blair, Nebraska. Eppley Airfield was hit by the third EF-3 tornado of the day, doing damage to aircraft hangars on the southeast side of the airport before hitting homes in far western Pottawattamie County. The last two EF-3 tornadoes of the day both impacted portions of Pottawattamie County. While one remained primarily in rural areas in the central part of the county, the final EF-3 of the day hit the town of Minden, Iowa head-on, resulting in 1 fatality and 3 injuries. This was the only fatality of the entire event. This tornado continued into Shelby County, narrowly missing the towns of Tenant and Harlan. A total of 24 tornadoes were confirmed across both Nebraska and Iowa portions of the Omaha CWA. The strongest tornado was the EF-3 that impacted Elkhorn and Blair, with peak estimated wind speeds at 165 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed near the intersection of 260th and 270th Streets. It produced damage to outbuildings at a farmstead near this intersection and tossed some fiberglass boats. The tornado moved across agricultural fields generally northeast paralleling State Highway 183 to the west. It produced damage to trees along Redwood Avenue and snapped a power pole at the intersection of State Highway 37 and Redwood Avenue. The tornado moved just west of the community of Soldier, producing no discernable damage to any homes or businesses on the west side of town. Radar signatures indicate the tornado continued to move northeast across agricultural lands, moving north of 245th Street, damaging trees and outbuildings, before dissipating just east of Spruce Avenue. Peak winds in this tornado were estimated at 98 miles per hour. The maximum width was measured at 250 yards, with the average width estimated at 100 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the Minden, Iowa EF-3 tornado. This tornado briefly crossed into the far southeast corner of Harrison County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded shortwave passed across Iowa in the afternoon, providing a focus for convection as it phased with an increasing southerly moisture stream. Steep lapse rates, plenty of instability, and hodographs supporting rotating updrafts all came together to create an environment supportive of hail producing supercells. Storms remained somewhat discrete and produced swaths of hail across central and southern Iowa. The largest hail reported was up to baseball sized in Appanoose County. A tornado also occurred in Wayne County east of Millerton with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter relayed copious amounts of hail, up to quarter size. Just west of Osceola and Truro. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms lined up across central Iowa on the 850 mb moisture gradient early in the morning of July 31, producing up to quarter sized hail and gusts over 50 mph in isolated downbursts. Storms shifted into southeast Iowa through the morning and remained strong, producing hail and strong winds. As storms shifted southeast the orientation shifted to allow for training storms will heavy rain of 3-6 inches in some locations. This area also received heavier rain the previous day when multiple rounds of storms moved across the area, so soils were less able to handle the additional rain. Areas of flash flooding resulting in road closures were reported. After a break in the afternoon and early evening, a bowing line of storms passed across central to southern Iowa. This brough another round of strong winds of 55 to 65 mph and isolated hail up to quarter sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of an MCV that tracked across Nebraska earlier in the day moved into Iowa in the late afternoon. These remnants moved into a deeply unstable environment with 2000+ J/kg of SBCAPE and intensified through the early evening, transitioning to a loosely organized bowing segment in the evening. Steep mid and low level lapse rates and DCAPE of 750-1000 J/kg were also available. This combination resulted in a handful of hail and damaging wind gusts across the area.  Heavy rain also occurred as a line of training convection developed behind the bowing segment lasting late into the night. A swath of 3 to 5 inches of rain occurred with the system in central to southern Iowa, however no flood impacts were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with large tree branch down. Time estimated by social media time and radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A PV anomaly entering the region and associated surface boundary interactions, with increasing forcing overhead and favorable deep layer shear, resulted in scattered on and off showers and thunderstorms throughout much of the day. This resulted in a handful of severe wind reports and isolated wind damage, particularly in Clarke and Marshall counties with gusts generally ranging between 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage has been reported with this wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant, and at times record, river flooding in northwest Iowa continued from June into July. Flooding resulted in massive sandbagging efforts, prolonged road closures and damages, and impacts to home and businesses. Continued heavy rain in early July kept some locations in flood through the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Stratford remained in flood from a peak crest in June 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN.  An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail varied in size from pea to ping-pong ball size.

Long standing abnormally dry conditions resulted in ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought affecting all counties in northeast Iowa from June 2023 to the onset of May 2024. A steady wetting pattern over the winter months into spring slowly alleviated extreme (D3) drought across northeast Iowa through April. Monthly rainfall through May finished 3 to 5 inches above normal, reducing all ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions to moderate (D1) through the middle of May and eventually ending the drought by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal total monthly precipitation near 10 inches resulted in reduction of severe (D2) drought to moderate (D1) through May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 6th and the 7th of the month, a longwave trough was centered over the central CONUS. At the surface, several wandering surface boundaries initiated isolated convection the afternoons and evenings of the 6th, 7th and 8th. A few of these storms produced funnel clouds which never touched the ground, though they garnered a lot of social media attention. Isolated instances of strong winds and severe hail were also noted across parts of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prevailing upper-level pattern for the end of July featured a trough over the Pacific Northwest, favoring southwesterly flow over the central Great Plains. Two troughs ejected out into the north-central CONUS during this episode. The first brought two back to back nights of severe weather across portions of northeast Nebraska the night of the 28th and the 29th. The second trough moved out into the central Plains and brought the synoptic scale forcing for a historic severe wind event that impacted both Lincoln and Omaha metropolitan areas. Wind gusts as high as 100 miles per hour extended from Lancaster County northeast to the Nebraska-Iowa border, leaving behind a large swath of significant tree, powerline and structural damage. Power outages from this storm lasted for up to a week in some locations, particularly in Omaha. ||This episode also featured several afternoons of excessive heat for portions of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. A few instances of 110 degree heat indices were observed in a few southwest Iowa counties the afternoon of the 29th, with even more widespread excessive heat the afternoon of the 31st across much of the forecast area south of Interstate 80. Peak heat indices in this region topped out between 110 and 115 degrees before afternoon convection cooled temperatures back down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of hot afternoon temperatures and high dew points brought a brief period of excessive heat during the afternoon, with heat indices up to 110 being observed in a few counties across southwest Iowa. The Clarinda AWOS station had artificially high heat indices, but was still likely meeting excessive heat criteria given its proximity to the Shenandoah AWOs station. As such, to be consistent with surrounding areas, heat indices up to 110 were still likely observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another wet period during the mid to later part of May drove portions of the upper and middle Little Sioux Basin back above flood stage after only briefly falling below flood stage just a handful of days early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Sioux River at Cherokee rose above flood stage the morning of May 22nd, cresting around 1.38 feet above flood stage (18.38 feet) on May 22nd. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of May 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another wet period during the mid to later part of May drove portions of the upper and middle Little Sioux Basin back above flood stage after only briefly falling below flood stage just a handful of days early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Willow Creek near Cornell rose above flood stage the evening of May 21st, cresting around 1.70 feet above flood stage (91.7 feet) on May 22nd. The river then fell below flood stage on the morning of May 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale bow echo developed across Nebraska in the late night hours and pushed northeast toward the lower Missouri River valley and northwest Iowa.  Winds associated with the convective line were as high as around 70 mph in the Sioux City metro area, and caused considerable tree damage and power outages to around 2000 customers. The roof on one home in the Morningside area of Sioux City was damaged by a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees and large branches along Floyd Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lasting complex of severe thunderstorms, known as a derecho, tracked from central Nebraska through northwest Illinois during the late night hours May 23rd, to the morning of May 24th. This resulted in frequent wind damage reports and embedded tornadoes. There were 16 tornadoes confirmed in the NWS Quad Cities warning area. The four strongest tornadoes all had maximum winds near 110 mph (EF1), and those were the Delta, IA; Wellman to Frytown, IA; Bennett to Lowden, IA; and Coyne Center, IL tornadoes. This was the most tornadoes in a single event for the NWS Quad Cities warning area since the March 31, 2023 outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed southeast of Dixon in Scott County and moved northeast into Clinton County. The intermittent tornado path was around 15 miles, to just east of Grand Mound, before lifting near Highway 61. The tornado caused minor tree and power pole damage, as well as damage to an outbuilding. The tornado was rated EF-1 with max winds around 90 mph. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of an MCV that tracked across Nebraska earlier in the day moved into Iowa in the late afternoon. These remnants moved into a deeply unstable environment with 2000+ J/kg of SBCAPE and intensified through the early evening, transitioning to a loosely organized bowing segment in the evening. Steep mid and low level lapse rates and DCAPE of 750-1000 J/kg were also available. This combination resulted in a handful of hail and damaging wind gusts across the area.  Heavy rain also occurred as a line of training convection developed behind the bowing segment lasting late into the night. A swath of 3 to 5 inches of rain occurred with the system in central to southern Iowa, however no flood impacts were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station reported 4.02 inches storm total with rain still in progress.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN.  An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.

Multiple rounds of thunderstorms during the May 19th through 21st timeframe produced areas of heavy rainfall across the region. As much as 2 to 4 inches of rain fell during the 3 day period. Ponding of water in fields and low lying areas was common with largely saturated soils already in place prior to the additional rainfall. Some gravel roads were also inundated, mainly across northern Buena Vista County. Rises along waterways through the Little Sioux River Basin also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms, along with already saturated ground, resulted in significant ponding of water in fields and low lying areas. A few county gravel roads were also briefly inundated after the heaviest rain the afternoon of May 21st requiring minor repair to the gravel surface. Water was also reported in some basements in the Linn Grove area due to a high water table. No reports of damage or impacts to structures from surface water flooding were received. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long standing abnormally dry conditions resulted in ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought affecting all counties in northeast Iowa from June 2023 to the onset of May 2024. A steady wetting pattern over the winter months into spring slowly alleviated extreme (D3) drought across northeast Iowa through April. Monthly rainfall through May finished 3 to 5 inches above normal, reducing all ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions to moderate (D1) through the middle of May and eventually ending the drought by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal total monthly precipitation near 10 inches resulted in reduction of severe (D2) drought to moderate (D1) through May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet last week of April resulted in saturated conditions and rising stream levels across portions of northwest Iowa. This helped push the Upper Little Sioux River between Milford and Spencer into Minor flood stage at the end of April which continued into May. These high flows translated downstream toward Linn Grove and Cherokee into the first week of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the Little Sioux River near Spencer continued from late April, cresting around 3.34 feet above flood stage (13.34 feet) on May 10th. The river then briefly fell below flood stage the afternoon of May 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 12 UTC on the 10th, a trough was positioned over the upper Midwest, with northwesterly flow over the Great Plains. At the surface, a surface trough extended from north-central Kansas into southeastern Iowa. In the afternoon hours, scattered thunderstorms developed across far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. While most of these storms remained below severe limits, a handful of severe hail reports were received by the Omaha National Weather Service.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prevailing upper-level pattern for the end of July featured a trough over the Pacific Northwest, favoring southwesterly flow over the central Great Plains. Two troughs ejected out into the north-central CONUS during this episode. The first brought two back to back nights of severe weather across portions of northeast Nebraska the night of the 28th and the 29th. The second trough moved out into the central Plains and brought the synoptic scale forcing for a historic severe wind event that impacted both Lincoln and Omaha metropolitan areas. Wind gusts as high as 100 miles per hour extended from Lancaster County northeast to the Nebraska-Iowa border, leaving behind a large swath of significant tree, powerline and structural damage. Power outages from this storm lasted for up to a week in some locations, particularly in Omaha. ||This episode also featured several afternoons of excessive heat for portions of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. A few instances of 110 degree heat indices were observed in a few southwest Iowa counties the afternoon of the 29th, with even more widespread excessive heat the afternoon of the 31st across much of the forecast area south of Interstate 80. Peak heat indices in this region topped out between 110 and 115 degrees before afternoon convection cooled temperatures back down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of hot temperatures and high dew points brought excessive heat during the afternoon hours. Heat indices climbed as high as 112 degrees for portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong negatively tilted trough lifted northward through the Western Plains during the afternoon and evening of May 6. While instability was not intense with around 1000 J/kg MLCAPE, a very strong low-level jet produced a considerable amount of boundary parallel deep-layer shear to entice a quick linear upscale growth, but the southwest-oriented shear in the 0-3km layer also led to brief mesovortices with enhanced wind corridors in segments which became oriented more northwest to southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant roof damage to outbuildings was caused by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms redeveloped near an area of low pressure and advancing frontal boundary across northwest Iowa. Storms in the dry slot produced gusty wind while organizing into a bowing line, with several reports of brief funnel clouds and one location along the line spinning up a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms redeveloped near an area of low pressure and advancing frontal boundary across northwest Iowa. Storms in the dry slot produced gusty wind while organizing into a bowing line, with several reports of brief funnel clouds and one location along the line spinning up a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado spun up just south of IA Highway 3 and west of 70th Avenue. The tornado tracked north-northeast for around 0.2 mile before removing roof panels from an outbuilding and tearing several limbs off a large tree. The tornado crossed Highway 3 where it snapped the tops from several hardwood trees. The tornado tracked for another half mile across open fields before impacting a farmstead where it removed the roof from an older residence and mangled several trees. The attached garage was shifted 6 to 10 inches off its foundation. The tornado then took a slightly more northeastward track over the next half mile, crossed 520th Street to another farm where it blew doors out of a garage, damaged empty grain bins and broke numerous tree limbs. The tornado lifted after another 0.3 mile across an open field. The estimated peak wind was 110 mph and the average path width was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure with an associated warm front tracked over northern Iowa along with another warm front that lifted into near western/southern Iowa during the morning hours, which resulted in moderate to heavy rainfall over portions of the state. Scattered showers and storms continued across much of Iowa throughout the day, with PWATS around 2-2.5 inches, a present LLJ and deeper warm cloud depths, leading to continued heavy rain. Rainfall totals up to 5 inches were noted primarily in Warren County, though Flash Flooding occurred in Marion and Mahaska counties into the early evening. Severe weather also was possible with damaging winds, hail and a low tornado potential over southern Iowa, though no severe reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall total since 8am CDT on July 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms impacted Iowa on July 30th. This first round occurred through the early morning as a bowing line of storms moved into the area from Nebraska around 1 am CDT. This passed across the area through the early morning hours, exiting into southeastern Iowa by 5 am. Several sites reported wind gusts of 60-65 mph as this line of storms swept across the area. A second bowing segment passed across the are in the mid to late morning, this time bringing hail along with the winds. In its wake, thunderstorms developed on the outflow boundary across central to southeast Iowa. These storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain along with a number of quarter sized and larger hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded a 66 mph gust.

Upper-level flow the evening of the 13th features a low-amplitude shortwave trough that had ejected out into the northern Great Plains. At the surface, a weakening stationary front was noted on WPC surface analysis to extend from northwest Nebraska into southeast Nebraska. Isolated strong thunderstorms that developed along this boundary brought a single severe wind gust in Knox County. However, the primary impact of this episode was the excessive heat that impacted much of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Heat indices the afternoon of the 15th in this area peaked between 110 and 115 degrees. The highest heat index was 117 degrees, observed by an AWOS station in Harlan, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early overnight hours across northwest Iowa ahead of a well-organized linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). Strong updrafts in the relatively isolated storms produced hail up to 2 inches in diameter, mainly near and south of the Iowa Great Lakes area.

A surface boundary stalled during the afternoon across northwest Iowa. With fairly narrow instability profiles but enough moisture to produce a very low lifting condensation level (LCL) along with a concentration of near-surface vorticity, updrafts produced a couple of bring non-supercell spin ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed on the far eastern edge of Sheldon near the Crossroads Pavilion. The tornado tracked north-northwest for around one-quarter mile and crossed U.S. Highway 18 at the intersection of 34th Avenue. North of Highway 18, the tornado impacted an RV dealership where the roof was partially peeled off a machine shed and several RVs were flipped or destroyed. Several roof panels of two other buildings were also peeled off. Debris was strewn across an open field to the northwest over the next one-third mile before the tornado crossed Nest Avenue shortly before it lifted. The maximum estimated wind speed was 85 mph and the average path width was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary stalled during the afternoon across northwest Iowa. With fairly narrow instability profiles but enough moisture to produce a very low lifting condensation level (LCL) along with a concentration of near-surface vorticity, updrafts produced a couple of bring non-supercell spin ups.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed around 3 miles southwest of Granville just east of Lily Avenue and north of 490th Street. The tornado moved generally north-northeast and near the end of the path, the tornado peeled pack the roof panels of an old outbuilding on a farmstead.  The tornado lifted before crossing 480th Street. The maximum estimated wind speed was 70 mph and the average path width was 25 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure with an associated warm front tracked over northern Iowa along with another warm front that lifted into near western/southern Iowa during the morning hours, which resulted in moderate to heavy rainfall over portions of the state. Scattered showers and storms continued across much of Iowa throughout the day, with PWATS around 2-2.5 inches, a present LLJ and deeper warm cloud depths, leading to continued heavy rain. Rainfall totals up to 5 inches were noted primarily in Warren County, though Flash Flooding occurred in Marion and Mahaska counties into the early evening. Severe weather also was possible with damaging winds, hail and a low tornado potential over southern Iowa, though no severe reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Car stranded in water on the road, with water over halfway up vehicles wheels. Estimated height of around 12 inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remains of an MCV that tracked across Nebraska earlier in the day moved into Iowa in the late afternoon. These remnants moved into a deeply unstable environment with 2000+ J/kg of SBCAPE and intensified through the early evening, transitioning to a loosely organized bowing segment in the evening. Steep mid and low level lapse rates and DCAPE of 750-1000 J/kg were also available. This combination resulted in a handful of hail and damaging wind gusts across the area.  Heavy rain also occurred as a line of training convection developed behind the bowing segment lasting late into the night. A swath of 3 to 5 inches of rain occurred with the system in central to southern Iowa, however no flood impacts were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station reported 4.48 inches of rain since midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale bow echo developed across Nebraska in the late night hours and pushed northeast toward the lower Missouri River valley and northwest Iowa.  Winds associated with the convective line were as high as around 70 mph in the Sioux City metro area, and caused considerable tree damage and power outages to around 2000 customers. The roof on one home in the Morningside area of Sioux City was damaged by a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust was measured by the Automated Surface Observing System site KSUX at Sioux Gateway Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms impacted Iowa on July 30th. This first round occurred through the early morning as a bowing line of storms moved into the area from Nebraska around 1 am CDT. This passed across the area through the early morning hours, exiting into southeastern Iowa by 5 am. Several sites reported wind gusts of 60-65 mph as this line of storms swept across the area. A second bowing segment passed across the are in the mid to late morning, this time bringing hail along with the winds. In its wake, thunderstorms developed on the outflow boundary across central to southeast Iowa. These storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain along with a number of quarter sized and larger hail reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level flow the evening of the 13th features a low-amplitude shortwave trough that had ejected out into the northern Great Plains. At the surface, a weakening stationary front was noted on WPC surface analysis to extend from northwest Nebraska into southeast Nebraska. Isolated strong thunderstorms that developed along this boundary brought a single severe wind gust in Knox County. However, the primary impact of this episode was the excessive heat that impacted much of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Heat indices the afternoon of the 15th in this area peaked between 110 and 115 degrees. The highest heat index was 117 degrees, observed by an AWOS station in Harlan, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices up to 113 impacted portions of the county for a few hours during the afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report of large limbs approximately 6-8 inches in diameter down on a home and power lines in Ventura. Time of occurrence estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long standing abnormally dry conditions resulted in ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought affecting all counties in northeast Iowa from June 2023 to the onset of May 2024. A steady wetting pattern over the winter months into spring slowly alleviated extreme (D3) drought across northeast Iowa through April. Monthly rainfall through May finished 3 to 5 inches above normal, reducing all ongoing severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions to moderate (D1) through the middle of May and eventually ending the drought by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal total monthly precipitation near 10 inches resulted in reduction of severe (D2) drought to moderate (D1) through May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing warm and moist air into Iowa, along with a shortwave arriving into the afternoon to evening and developing LLJ led to the development of areas of showers and thunderstorms. Favorable shear and instability parameters overhead led to at least a few severe thunderstorms, generally occurring over northern into parts of central Iowa. In particular, Humboldt county down to Story county experienced severe hail ranging between 1-1.5 inches, largest in the town of Bode, as well as thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shredded leaves with some dented cars occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN.  An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.

Heavy rain also occurred as a line of training convection developed behind the bowing segment lasting late into the night. A swath of 3 to 5 inches of rain occurred with the system in central to southern Iowa, however no flood impacts were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rain total of 7.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following showers and thunderstorms over Iowa during the morning hours with a passing shortwave, an additional shortwave arriving in the Central Plains with the aid of increased lift and MUCAPE values around 1000 J/kg overhead brought another round of scattered showers and storms into the afternoon and evening hours. These multiple rounds of convection, along with the slow-moving nature of some storms led to heavy rainfall and eventually flash flooding, particularly over Wright county. Reports of flooded roads and rainfall totals averaging between 3-5 inches were common. Severe wind was also reported in Adair county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of water over the road on Highway 17 near Eagle Grove. Local city roads in Eagle Grove as well as Clarion are reportedly flooded. Time of occurrence estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early overnight hours across northwest Iowa ahead of a well-organized linear mesoscale convective system (MCS). Strong updrafts in the relatively isolated storms produced hail up to 2 inches in diameter, mainly near and south of the Iowa Great Lakes area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing warm and moist air into Iowa, along with a shortwave arriving into the afternoon to evening and developing LLJ led to the development of areas of showers and thunderstorms. Favorable shear and instability parameters overhead led to at least a few severe thunderstorms, generally occurring over northern into parts of central Iowa. In particular, Humboldt county down to Story county experienced severe hail ranging between 1-1.5 inches, largest in the town of Bode, as well as thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Via WHO-TV of 1.5-inch hail in Eagle Grove. Time of occurrence estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated convection across northeast Nebraska brought an instance of flash flooding in Cedar County where a trained spotter reported water flowing over multiple county roads. There was also a funnel cloud the following day that received a lot of social media attention that developed over Norfolk. Additional funnels clouds were noted in Monona County the evening of the 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The office received a delayed report of a rope funnel cloud from the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet last week of April resulted in saturated conditions and rising stream levels across portions of northwest Iowa. This helped push the Upper Little Sioux River between Milford and Spencer into Minor flood stage at the end of April which continued into May. These high flows translated downstream toward Linn Grove and Cherokee into the first week of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ocheyedan River near Spencer rose above flood stage the morning of May 2nd, cresting around 2.16 feet above flood stage (10.16 feet) on May 9th. This crest is in the Moderate Stage. The river then fell below flood stage on the evening of May 12th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on the Little Sioux River near Milford continued from late April, cresting around 2.28 feet above flood stage (14.28 feet) on May 9th. This crest is in the Moderate Stage. The river then fell below flood stage during the early morning of May 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours produced pockets of heavy rainfall and very efficient rainfall rates. This resulted in localized flash flooding reports across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa. Even after the heaviest rainfall rates had ended, a stripe of prolonged moderate rainfall resulted in excessive ponding and some minor flooding of rural roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of thunderstorms, followed by multiple hours of persistent moderate rainfall, resulted in excessive ponding of water in fields and rural roads. This included water over the road near the intersection of Nest Ave and 310th St. Rainfall totals through the entire event were reported between 2 to 4.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms through the morning dissipated for a few hours around midday. By late afternoon additional thunderstorms developed along a boundary draped across Iowa from northwest to southeast. A weak upper level shortwave provided enough lift to initiate storms along this boundary. With deep layer shear of 30-35 kts, storms were able to develop and organize. Most storms remained sub-severe, however one storm just south of the Des Moines metro produced quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing warm air advection supported scattered convection, however the lack of surface boundary and modest parameter space prevented storms from becoming organized. A few storms developed across far southern Iowa in the warm air advection wing in the early evening. While these largely remained sub-severe,  a single storm did produce a 59 mph gust at the Lamoni ASOS. Based on the radar signature, the environment with increasing dry air aloft, and isolated nature of the report, it is suspected that this was a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lamoni ASOS reported a gust of 59 mph. Likely a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prevailing upper-level pattern for the end of July featured a trough over the Pacific Northwest, favoring southwesterly flow over the central Great Plains. Two troughs ejected out into the north-central CONUS during this episode. The first brought two back to back nights of severe weather across portions of northeast Nebraska the night of the 28th and the 29th. The second trough moved out into the central Plains and brought the synoptic scale forcing for a historic severe wind event that impacted both Lincoln and Omaha metropolitan areas. Wind gusts as high as 100 miles per hour extended from Lancaster County northeast to the Nebraska-Iowa border, leaving behind a large swath of significant tree, powerline and structural damage. Power outages from this storm lasted for up to a week in some locations, particularly in Omaha. ||This episode also featured several afternoons of excessive heat for portions of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. A few instances of 110 degree heat indices were observed in a few southwest Iowa counties the afternoon of the 29th, with even more widespread excessive heat the afternoon of the 31st across much of the forecast area south of Interstate 80. Peak heat indices in this region topped out between 110 and 115 degrees before afternoon convection cooled temperatures back down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Department of Highways station measured a 61 miles per hour wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A PV anomaly entering the region and associated surface boundary interactions, with increasing forcing overhead and favorable deep layer shear, resulted in scattered on and off showers and thunderstorms throughout much of the day. This resulted in a handful of severe wind reports and isolated wind damage, particularly in Clarke and Marshall counties with gusts generally ranging between 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured wind gust of 62mph, with 1 to 2 inch limbs broken off trees. Nearly an inch of rain in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe storms produced quarter sized hail in Winneshiek County during the late afternoon and evening hours on May 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported by the public northeast of Decorah. Report relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of May 9th, multiple sources reported a landspout tornado associated with a weak thunderstorm south of Charles City, IA. No damage was reported with this landspout tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources reported a very short lived non-supercell tornado south of Charles City, IA. Video footage showed a brief touchdown in an open field. No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale bow echo developed across Nebraska in the late night hours and pushed northeast toward the lower Missouri River valley and northwest Iowa.  Winds associated with the convective line were as high as around 70 mph in the Sioux City metro area, and caused considerable tree damage and power outages to around 2000 customers. The roof on one home in the Morningside area of Sioux City was damaged by a falling tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured by a personal weather station, with damage to tree limbs also noted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms redeveloped near an area of low pressure and advancing frontal boundary across northwest Iowa. Storms in the dry slot produced gusty wind while organizing into a bowing line, with several reports of brief funnel clouds and one location along the line spinning up a weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a wooden power pole, which caused failure of several adjacent poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant, and at times record, river flooding in northwest Iowa continued from June into July. Flooding resulted in massive sandbagging efforts, prolonged road closures and damages, and impacts to home and businesses. Continued heavy rain in early July kept some locations in flood through the middle of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Des Moines River at Fort Dodge remained in flood from a peak crest in June 2024.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: While the greatest measured wind gust in Pottawattamie County was 70 miles per hour, recorded by a mesonet station 4 miles southwest of McClelland, winds were estimated to have been as high as 90 miles per hour entering the west side of the county, in accordance with the 90 miles per hour wind gust measured by the Eppley Airfield ASOS. This line of severe thunderstorms weakened substantially across the county, with the last incident of severe wind damage reported out of Oakland where a power line was blown down and a tree fell onto a house. A semi was also reported blown over on I-29 southwest of Crescent, scattered instances of downed trees and power lines were also noted across Council Bluffs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe storms impacted Iowa on July 30th. This first round occurred through the early morning as a bowing line of storms moved into the area from Nebraska around 1 am CDT. This passed across the area through the early morning hours, exiting into southeastern Iowa by 5 am. Several sites reported wind gusts of 60-65 mph as this line of storms swept across the area. A second bowing segment passed across the are in the mid to late morning, this time bringing hail along with the winds. In its wake, thunderstorms developed on the outflow boundary across central to southeast Iowa. These storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain along with a number of quarter sized and larger hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple branches up to 6 inches down across town with lone larger 12 inch branch down. Flattened corn outside of town. Delayed report, time estimated via radar.

This first round occurred through the early morning as a bowing line of storms moved into the area from Nebraska around 1 am CDT. This passed across the area through the early morning hours, exiting into southeastern Iowa by 5 am. Several sites reported wind gusts of 60-65 mph as this line of storms swept across the area. A second bowing segment passed across the are in the mid to late morning, this time bringing hail along with the winds. In its wake, thunderstorms developed on the outflow boundary across central to southeast Iowa. These storms were slow moving and produced heavy rain along with a number of quarter sized and larger hail reports.

The prevailing upper-level pattern for the end of July featured a trough over the Pacific Northwest, favoring southwesterly flow over the central Great Plains. Two troughs ejected out into the north-central CONUS during this episode. The first brought two back to back nights of severe weather across portions of northeast Nebraska the night of the 28th and the 29th. The second trough moved out into the central Plains and brought the synoptic scale forcing for a historic severe wind event that impacted both Lincoln and Omaha metropolitan areas. Wind gusts as high as 100 miles per hour extended from Lancaster County northeast to the Nebraska-Iowa border, leaving behind a large swath of significant tree, powerline and structural damage. Power outages from this storm lasted for up to a week in some locations, particularly in Omaha. ||This episode also featured several afternoons of excessive heat for portions of southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. A few instances of 110 degree heat indices were observed in a few southwest Iowa counties the afternoon of the 29th, with even more widespread excessive heat the afternoon of the 31st across much of the forecast area south of Interstate 80. Peak heat indices in this region topped out between 110 and 115 degrees before afternoon convection cooled temperatures back down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 miles per hour wind gust was measured on a personal weather station. Severe winds at this location persisted for almost 20 minutes, with a 58 miles per hour wind gust also being recorded on a mesonet station 17 minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) moved through the region on the morning of June 16. This quick moving system produced widespread winds between 60 to 70 mph through it's path, especially for areas along and east of the Mississippi River. Isolated reports of hail were noted, with the largest being golf ball sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 inch tree branch was downed by the thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unorganized line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed in eastern Iowa on the afternoon of June 3. This line of thunderstorms continued into northwest Illinois, with thunderstorms capable of severe winds and heavy rain. A quick 1-3 inches of rainfall fell for some, which resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured from a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long standing abnormally dry conditions were slightly alleviated in northeast Iowa through April. Besides a single week in July 2023, severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought had been affecting counties in northeast Iowa since June 6, 2023. From the beginning of June 2023 to April onset, total liquid precipitation of 15 to 20 was more than 9 below normal over the 10 month time period. While 3 to 5 of total monthly precipitation was near normal, several days of heavier liquid precipitation above 0.25 sufficiently saturated soils through April resulting in slight reduction of severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought conditions. The extreme (D3) drought conditions that affected Howard, Mitchell, and Floyd counties improved to severe (D2) drought while the extreme (D3) drought affecting Fayette and Chickasaw counties retreated slightly south. In far northeast Iowa along the Iowa and Minnesota border, severe drought receded southwest, relieving its hold on nearly all of Winneshiek and Allamakee counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slightly above normal monthly rainfall totals alleviated county wide extreme (D3) drought across Floyd County through April but severe (D2) drought persisted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief pulse of mid-level warm advection enhanced an area of light snow which moved across southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and evening hours. In just a few hours, snowfall as much as 3 to 5 inches fell in a fairly small area from just west of Sioux Falls southeast to Sioux City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.1 inches at Sioux Gateway Airport and 2.6 inches on the north side of Sioux City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on May 24th, a line of thunderstorms moved through northeast Iowa producing high wind gusts with Charles City reporting a peak wind gust of 62 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Charles City reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable air mass across the region ahead of a southward sinking cold front resulted in thunderstorm development, with several being severe. These produced large hail and scattered damaging winds. Most hail reports ranged in size from quarter to golf ball size hail. The largest hail fell in Van Buren County near Keosauqua where 2 to 3 inch diameter hail fell. There were also a few reports of 60 to 80 MPH across far southeast Iowa into west central Illinois, which damaged trees and knocked down several outbuildings.  The most widespread damage occurred near Oakville, IA.  Two brief, non-damaging tornadoes were also seen north of Macomb, IL.

A series of shortwave troughs traversing the northern CONUS brought unsettled weather to the area for the end of June. On the 21st, a stationary front was draped across northeast Nebraska into Iowa. Ahead of one of the aforementioned shortwave troughs, several rounds of strong thunderstorms developed near this frontal zone and moved across northeast Nebraska. At 8 pm on the 21st, radar imagery showed training thunderstorms had set up across central Nebraska. These storms moved into east-central Nebraska dropping 2 to 6 inches of rain across the Platte River Basin. As a result, minor flooding was observed along the Platte River in Dodge, Saunders, Douglas and Sarpy Counties. Further north in Knox County, additional rounds of thunderstorms brought flooding to the Ponca Creek, prompting emergency management to deploy sand bags. Flooding in these locations continued for several days before subsiding the 23rd.||Behind this first shortwave, a shortwave ridge moved out into the central Plains accompanied by a surface high pressure system. Afternoon high temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the mid and upper 70s brought excessive heat to much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa the afternoons of the 24th and the 25th. Excessive heat was most widespread on the 24th, with heat indices across the entire WFO ranging from 107 to 120 degrees. Afternoon thunderstorms on the 25th mitigated excessive heat, though heat indices did hit 110 in far southwest Iowa.||On the 25th, another shortwave trough brought more unsettled weather to the region. A surface front was draped across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa when the first convection initiated over downtown Omaha. This storm quickly became intense, producing multiple instances of 3 to 4 inch hail in the Omaha metro. As this storm weakened over eastern Sarpy County, additional strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Nebraska. While the most widespread hazard was large hail up to the size of golf balls, two tornadoes were confirmed in Madison County. Later that evening, a bow echo developed and moved through the northern Omaha Metro, bringing wind gusts up to 90 miles per hour at Eppley Airfield and into northern Pottawattamie County. An additional line of storms developed after dark that night across southeast Nebraska and produced scattered damaging wind gusts, the highest of which was an 83 mph wind gust measured by emergency management in Jefferson County.||The unsettled weather at the end of June brought widespread excessive rain to much of southeast South Dakota and Northwest Iowa. Rainfall estimated from these storms showed a swath of 4 to 8 inches of rain that fell between Sioux Falls and Sioux City. The result of this rain and additional rain across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa was river flooding along the Missouri and Little Sioux the last week of June and into July. Flooding during this time frame resulted in numerous road closures, including I-29 between Council Bluffs and Missouri Valley. This stretch of the interstate was closed beginning midnight the night of the 25th, and remained closed through the end of the month. Levee breaches in Monona County on the 25th prompted several evacuations, as resultant flash flooding inundated multiple county roads and homesteads. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this. While flooding subsided in and north of the Omaha metro by the end of the month, river flooding along the Missouri persisted into the first week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very slow moving supercell thunderstorm developed over downtown Omaha. After remaining stationary for about 30 minutes, it slowly started moving to the south. This storm began producing severe hail in a swath from Council Bluffs to eastern Sarpy County. Multiple reports of hail ranging from quarter to tennis ball sized were received by the office via social media and phone calls from the public and trained spotters.
