EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system brought a mix of heavy rain, snow, and strong winds to all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and northeast Missouri on the days of April 1- 3, 2024. Precipitation began as all rain during the morning of April 2nd, but quickly changed to a moderate to heavy snow by early afternoon as colder air moved into the area. Snow amounts between 2-4 inches were observed across portions of eastern Iowa, with widespread rainfall amounts of 1-3 inches.||Areas of light to moderate snow and rain continued into April 3rd, with snowfall totals around 3-5 inches mainly along and north of Highway 30 in eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. A snowfall amount of 6.8 inches was observed in eastern Dubuque county. In addition, strong northwest winds accompanied the system on April 3rd. Maximum wind gusts around 35-45 MPH were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports ranged from 2.5 inches 1 mile southeast of Dubuque to 6.8 inches 2miles east of King. The official snowfall observation at the Dubuque Regional Airport was 6.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of showers and thunderstorms during the day on April 27th as a pair of disturbances lifted northeastward across the area ahead of a cold front. These storms produced hail and damaging wind gusts as they moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a 70 MPH wind gust measured in town. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms with very high rainfall rates from 2 to 4 inches per hour developed by midday on June 20, and despite being transient, continued to redevelop through the evening and into the overnight hours. The primary focus occurred between Iowa Highway 9 and U.S. Highway 18 as the effective cold pool boundary collapsed southward and locked in place through the night. The final 24 hour rainfall totals reached 6 to 12 inches by daybreak across and near Lyon and northern Sioux counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another 3 to 5 inches of rainfall on top of a similar amount from the earlier afternoon resulted in water flowing across U.S. Highway 75 north of the junction of U.S. Highway 18. For a more comprehensive description of flood damage, please see other entries for areal and river flooding for Sioux County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More isolated afternoon and early evening storms erupted just north of a frontal boundary across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa, which produced up to quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph. Storms became more widespread during the evening given a mid-level impulse and increase in the low-level jet across the frontal boundary. The rain regenerated in many of the same areas as the previous night's excessive rainfall and brought two-day totals to as high as 8 to 16 inches setting several records. Widespread flash flooding developed during the evening and overnight hours with catastrophic damage in Rock Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees in Sibley and caused power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts across Dickinson County from June 20-22 ranged from 5 to 10 inches. Widespread overland flooding ensued with additional major to record flooding along the West Fork Little Sioux and Little Sioux Rivers. Three bridges were also damaged and closed. Dozens of bank collapses were reported along East Okoboji, West Okoboji, Big Spirit, and Silver Lakes with nearly 3,000 ft of shoreline lost. West Okoboji Lake crested about six inches below the record set in 1993. ||The majority of overland flooding subsided by June 25, but river flooding continued through the end of the month. For additional information on river flooding, please see other flood entries for Dickinson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a morning mesoscale convective system (MCS) across Nebraska, storms refired during the evening hours across northwest Iowa within fairly weak deep-layer shear and up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three cars stalled in high water ponded in city streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted late evening on June 16 along a slightly elevated boundary with 2500-3500 J/kg MUCAPE and 30-35 knots of deep-layer effective shear. Initial storms quickly became widespread along the boundary from south central to east central South Dakota and evolved into a large bow echo, which moved through southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa with numerous reports of damaging winds mainly along and south of Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two cars were damaged at one residence and at least two other homes have trees and branches down on the buildings with minor damage due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system brought a mix of heavy rain, snow, and strong winds to all of eastern Iowa, northwest Illinois and northeast Missouri on the days of April 1- 3, 2024. Precipitation began as all rain during the morning of April 2nd, but quickly changed to a moderate to heavy snow by early afternoon as colder air moved into the area. Snow amounts between 2-4 inches were observed across portions of eastern Iowa, with widespread rainfall amounts of 1-3 inches.||Areas of light to moderate snow and rain continued into April 3rd, with snowfall totals around 3-5 inches mainly along and north of Highway 30 in eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. A snowfall amount of 6.8 inches was observed in eastern Dubuque county. In addition, strong northwest winds accompanied the system on April 3rd. Maximum wind gusts around 35-45 MPH were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall report of 3.0 inches by the COOP Observer in Bellevue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave troughs traversing the northern CONUS brought unsettled weather to the area for the end of June. On the 21st, a stationary front was draped across northeast Nebraska into Iowa. Ahead of one of the aforementioned shortwave troughs, several rounds of strong thunderstorms developed near this frontal zone and moved across northeast Nebraska. At 8 pm on the 21st, radar imagery showed training thunderstorms had set up across central Nebraska. These storms moved into east-central Nebraska dropping 2 to 6 inches of rain across the Platte River Basin. As a result, minor flooding was observed along the Platte River in Dodge, Saunders, Douglas and Sarpy Counties. Further north in Knox County, additional rounds of thunderstorms brought flooding to the Ponca Creek, prompting emergency management to deploy sand bags. Flooding in these locations continued for several days before subsiding the 23rd.||Behind this first shortwave, a shortwave ridge moved out into the central Plains accompanied by a surface high pressure system. Afternoon high temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the mid and upper 70s brought excessive heat to much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa the afternoons of the 24th and the 25th. Excessive heat was most widespread on the 24th, with heat indices across the entire WFO ranging from 107 to 120 degrees. Afternoon thunderstorms on the 25th mitigated excessive heat, though heat indices did hit 110 in far southwest Iowa.||On the 25th, another shortwave trough brought more unsettled weather to the region. A surface front was draped across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa when the first convection initiated over downtown Omaha. This storm quickly became intense, producing multiple instances of 3 to 4 inch hail in the Omaha metro. As this storm weakened over eastern Sarpy County, additional strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Nebraska. While the most widespread hazard was large hail up to the size of golf balls, two tornadoes were confirmed in Madison County. Later that evening, a bow echo developed and moved through the northern Omaha Metro, bringing wind gusts up to 90 miles per hour at Eppley Airfield and into northern Pottawattamie County. An additional line of storms developed after dark that night across southeast Nebraska and produced scattered damaging wind gusts, the highest of which was an 83 mph wind gust measured by emergency management in Jefferson County.||The unsettled weather at the end of June brought widespread excessive rain to much of southeast South Dakota and Northwest Iowa. Rainfall estimated from these storms showed a swath of 4 to 8 inches of rain that fell between Sioux Falls and Sioux City. The result of this rain and additional rain across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa was river flooding along the Missouri and Little Sioux the last week of June and into July. Flooding during this time frame resulted in numerous road closures, including I-29 between Council Bluffs and Missouri Valley. This stretch of the interstate was closed beginning midnight the night of the 25th, and remained closed through the end of the month. Levee breaches in Monona County on the 25th prompted several evacuations, as resultant flash flooding inundated multiple county roads and homesteads. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this. While flooding subsided in and north of the Omaha metro by the end of the month, river flooding along the Missouri persisted into the first week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall across much of eastern South Dakota resulted in river flooding along the Missouri River at the end of June. Moderate flood stage was observed along the Missouri, with two Omaha river gauges cresting between 32.5 and 33.0 feet. Flood stage for this stretch of the Missouri ranges from 27.0 to 27.5 feet. At 3 pm, emergency management closed down Gifford Road in Council Bluffs as flood waters had reached the edge of the travel portion of the roadway. At 5:52 pm CDT on 6/25, Iowa Department of Transportation closed the I-680 Exit 1 westbound off-ramp as it became impassible due to flooding. By noon on 6/26, water was observed flowing over portions of the interstate and over lowlands into nearby farm fields. Later into the evening of the 26th, Iowa Department of Transportation reported numerous agricultural and local levee breaches west of Interstate 29 and north of Interstate 680 resulting in the rapid submerging of roads in this area. By 10:00 pm CDT, emergency management reported water to the shoulder of Interstate 29. By the morning of the 27th, water was observed on traffic cameras as covering segments of Interstate 29, primarily around mile marker 67.2 and north of Honey Creek. At 7:47 am CDT on the 29th, emergency management sent drone photos of I-29 completely submerged at and north of the I-680 interchange. Multiple ramps at the interchange were also submerged. At 3:30 pm CDT on the 29th, emergency management reported the waters had begun to recede, partially uncovering previously flooded stretches of Interstate 29. Damage in this county from the flood was estimated to be $637,250.00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN.  An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system in northwest flow traversed the edge of the arctic airmass, and brought a pulse of warm advection which resulted in a brief burst of moderate snowfall. Accumulations ranged from 1 to 5 inches, with some areas of blowing snow from northwest wind gusts as high as 20 to 30 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.1 inches at Spirit Lake and 3.0 inches at Okoboji. Visibility was occasionally as low as 3/4 mile at times of falling snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lasting complex of severe thunderstorms, known as a derecho, tracked from central Nebraska through northwest Illinois during the late night hours May 23rd, to the morning of May 24th. This resulted in frequent wind damage reports and embedded tornadoes. There were 16 tornadoes confirmed in the NWS Quad Cities warning area. The four strongest tornadoes all had maximum winds near 110 mph (EF1), and those were the Delta, IA; Wellman to Frytown, IA; Bennett to Lowden, IA; and Coyne Center, IL tornadoes. This was the most tornadoes in a single event for the NWS Quad Cities warning area since the March 31, 2023 outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed southwest of Wellman in Washington County, and tracked quickly to the northeast along a patch through the Amish and Frytown areas in Johnson County. The tornado caused damage to homes, outbuildings, and trees. No injuries were reported. Thanks to Washington and Johnson County emergency management for assistance in completing the survey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms moved through the area on Tuesday, May 21. The bulk of the severe weather occurred during the late afternoon and evening time frame, with several Severe Thunderstorm Warnings issued over eastern Iowa into northwestern Illinois.|Straight-line winds were the main hazard with the storms and impressive wind gusts were observed. The strongest gust reported was 100 mph, which was from a personal weather station located just southeast of Atkins, IA! There were also several other locations that reported wind gusts in excess of 70 mph, and damage consistent with that.

During the afternoon and evening hours of May 27th, a few severe storms moved through Iowa, Illinois, and northeast Missouri. While there were not many storms, they remained strong to severe for most of the entire path through the area, producing large hail reports for the most part, along with some damaging winds, and one isolated EF0 tornado. Most notably, these storms produced significant swaths of small hail, accumulating deep enough to affect roads and leave piles near homes that lasted hours. Some areas of crops were damaged by the small hail and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail, with also a large accumulation of small hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly unstable air mass gained ground near and to the east of an inverted trough which locked in place from central Nebraska into southwest MN.  An increase in warm advection into the region along with slightly stronger deep-layer effective shear up to 35 knots allowed for scattered and fairly discrete storms to develop, some of which became supercellular with very large hail. Hail up to 3 inches in diameter fell, with a few storms producing winds in excess of 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball to tennis ball sized hail caused damage to the skylight on a camper and downed numerous leaves and branches. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees, caused some siding damage and took several power lines down around Sergeant Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Compounded by earlier snowfall, whiteout and near whiteout conditions developed as northwest winds peaked at 41 mph at Marcus during the afternoon of January 13. Travel was not recommended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave troughs traversing the northern CONUS brought unsettled weather to the area for the end of June. On the 21st, a stationary front was draped across northeast Nebraska into Iowa. Ahead of one of the aforementioned shortwave troughs, several rounds of strong thunderstorms developed near this frontal zone and moved across northeast Nebraska. At 8 pm on the 21st, radar imagery showed training thunderstorms had set up across central Nebraska. These storms moved into east-central Nebraska dropping 2 to 6 inches of rain across the Platte River Basin. As a result, minor flooding was observed along the Platte River in Dodge, Saunders, Douglas and Sarpy Counties. Further north in Knox County, additional rounds of thunderstorms brought flooding to the Ponca Creek, prompting emergency management to deploy sand bags. Flooding in these locations continued for several days before subsiding the 23rd.||Behind this first shortwave, a shortwave ridge moved out into the central Plains accompanied by a surface high pressure system. Afternoon high temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the mid and upper 70s brought excessive heat to much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa the afternoons of the 24th and the 25th. Excessive heat was most widespread on the 24th, with heat indices across the entire WFO ranging from 107 to 120 degrees. Afternoon thunderstorms on the 25th mitigated excessive heat, though heat indices did hit 110 in far southwest Iowa.||On the 25th, another shortwave trough brought more unsettled weather to the region. A surface front was draped across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa when the first convection initiated over downtown Omaha. This storm quickly became intense, producing multiple instances of 3 to 4 inch hail in the Omaha metro. As this storm weakened over eastern Sarpy County, additional strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Nebraska. While the most widespread hazard was large hail up to the size of golf balls, two tornadoes were confirmed in Madison County. Later that evening, a bow echo developed and moved through the northern Omaha Metro, bringing wind gusts up to 90 miles per hour at Eppley Airfield and into northern Pottawattamie County. An additional line of storms developed after dark that night across southeast Nebraska and produced scattered damaging wind gusts, the highest of which was an 83 mph wind gust measured by emergency management in Jefferson County.||The unsettled weather at the end of June brought widespread excessive rain to much of southeast South Dakota and Northwest Iowa. Rainfall estimated from these storms showed a swath of 4 to 8 inches of rain that fell between Sioux Falls and Sioux City. The result of this rain and additional rain across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa was river flooding along the Missouri and Little Sioux the last week of June and into July. Flooding during this time frame resulted in numerous road closures, including I-29 between Council Bluffs and Missouri Valley. This stretch of the interstate was closed beginning midnight the night of the 25th, and remained closed through the end of the month. Levee breaches in Monona County on the 25th prompted several evacuations, as resultant flash flooding inundated multiple county roads and homesteads. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this. While flooding subsided in and north of the Omaha metro by the end of the month, river flooding along the Missouri persisted into the first week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A max heat index of 118 was observed at the Council Bluffs AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several rounds of severe storms on May 8th, 2024. An early morning line of storms brought wind damage to a large portion of Iowa, Illinois, and northeast Missouri.  In the early afternoon, a few storms brought large hail to northwest Illinois, followed by another round of gusty storms during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Siding on home damaged and tree branches blown down. The time was based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lasting complex of severe thunderstorms, known as a derecho, tracked from central Nebraska through northwest Illinois during the late night hours May 23rd, to the morning of May 24th. This resulted in frequent wind damage reports and embedded tornadoes. There were 16 tornadoes confirmed in the NWS Quad Cities warning area. The four strongest tornadoes all had maximum winds near 110 mph (EF1), and those were the Delta, IA; Wellman to Frytown, IA; Bennett to Lowden, IA; and Coyne Center, IL tornadoes. This was the most tornadoes in a single event for the NWS Quad Cities warning area since the March 31, 2023 outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a tornado north of Sutliff in far southeast Linn County. Public video also showed a tornado south and east of Mechanicsville, IA. There was no known damage to assign a rating, so the tornado is classified as an EF-Unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms moved through the area on Tuesday, May 21. The bulk of the severe weather occurred during the late afternoon and evening time frame, with several Severe Thunderstorm Warnings issued over eastern Iowa into northwestern Illinois.|Straight-line winds were the main hazard with the storms and impressive wind gusts were observed. The strongest gust reported was 100 mph, which was from a personal weather station located just southeast of Atkins, IA! There were also several other locations that reported wind gusts in excess of 70 mph, and damage consistent with that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported via Mping.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -53 degrees 5 miles southeast of Spencer and -47 degrees at Spencer during the evening of January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-faceted winter system impacted much of the area from the afternoon hours of January 11 through early on January 16.||Initial wintry impacts resulted from snowfall which developed and wrapped northward through areas along and south of I-90 during the afternoon and early evening of the 11th and ended during the afternoon on the 12th. Periods of very low visibility in moderate to briefly heavy snow occurred mainly south of U.S. Highway 18 during the morning and early afternoon of the 12th, but winds remained below blizzard thresholds. As a result, snow accumulations generally varied from a trace to light dusting north of I-90, up to an inch or two along I-90, and up to 3 to 8 inches south of U.S. Highway 18.||As the snow tapered off on the 12th, northwest winds increased to a gusty 35 to 55 mph, which ushered in a period of blowing snow and bitterly cold wind chills. Where greater accumulating snow occurred on the 11th and 12th, winds produced significant ground blizzard conditions through much of the day on the 13th as winds generally peaked. Roads were either closed or travel not advised across northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, as well as areas of southeast South Dakota near and south of I-90 from the 13th into the 14th. Winds diminished some over the following couple of days, but as temperatures fell into the teens to around 20 below zero and remained below zero for up to 72 to 84 hours, wind chill values reached 40 below to 60 below zero at times. The coldest values occurred during the period of stronger winds on the 13th to early on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills remained -35 degrees or colder at times over a nearly four-day period, with a minimum wind chill of -51 degrees at Terril, -49 degrees at Milford, and -46 degrees near Spirit Lake and Lake Park during the evening of January 13 and early overnight January 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwave troughs traversing the northern CONUS brought unsettled weather to the area for the end of June. On the 21st, a stationary front was draped across northeast Nebraska into Iowa. Ahead of one of the aforementioned shortwave troughs, several rounds of strong thunderstorms developed near this frontal zone and moved across northeast Nebraska. At 8 pm on the 21st, radar imagery showed training thunderstorms had set up across central Nebraska. These storms moved into east-central Nebraska dropping 2 to 6 inches of rain across the Platte River Basin. As a result, minor flooding was observed along the Platte River in Dodge, Saunders, Douglas and Sarpy Counties. Further north in Knox County, additional rounds of thunderstorms brought flooding to the Ponca Creek, prompting emergency management to deploy sand bags. Flooding in these locations continued for several days before subsiding the 23rd.||Behind this first shortwave, a shortwave ridge moved out into the central Plains accompanied by a surface high pressure system. Afternoon high temperatures in the 90s and dew points in the mid and upper 70s brought excessive heat to much of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa the afternoons of the 24th and the 25th. Excessive heat was most widespread on the 24th, with heat indices across the entire WFO ranging from 107 to 120 degrees. Afternoon thunderstorms on the 25th mitigated excessive heat, though heat indices did hit 110 in far southwest Iowa.||On the 25th, another shortwave trough brought more unsettled weather to the region. A surface front was draped across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa when the first convection initiated over downtown Omaha. This storm quickly became intense, producing multiple instances of 3 to 4 inch hail in the Omaha metro. As this storm weakened over eastern Sarpy County, additional strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeast Nebraska. While the most widespread hazard was large hail up to the size of golf balls, two tornadoes were confirmed in Madison County. Later that evening, a bow echo developed and moved through the northern Omaha Metro, bringing wind gusts up to 90 miles per hour at Eppley Airfield and into northern Pottawattamie County. An additional line of storms developed after dark that night across southeast Nebraska and produced scattered damaging wind gusts, the highest of which was an 83 mph wind gust measured by emergency management in Jefferson County.||The unsettled weather at the end of June brought widespread excessive rain to much of southeast South Dakota and Northwest Iowa. Rainfall estimated from these storms showed a swath of 4 to 8 inches of rain that fell between Sioux Falls and Sioux City. The result of this rain and additional rain across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa was river flooding along the Missouri and Little Sioux the last week of June and into July. Flooding during this time frame resulted in numerous road closures, including I-29 between Council Bluffs and Missouri Valley. This stretch of the interstate was closed beginning midnight the night of the 25th, and remained closed through the end of the month. Levee breaches in Monona County on the 25th prompted several evacuations, as resultant flash flooding inundated multiple county roads and homesteads. No injuries or fatalities were reported from this. While flooding subsided in and north of the Omaha metro by the end of the month, river flooding along the Missouri persisted into the first week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall across much of eastern South Dakota resulted in river flooding along the Missouri River at the end of June. Moderate flood stage was observed along the Missouri, with the river gauge in Nebraska City cresting at 24.35 feet on 6/30. Flood stage at this river gauge is 18.0 feet. The county did not report any damages to the State of Iowa, so have left damages at zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a morning mesoscale convective system (MCS) across Nebraska, storms refired during the evening hours across northwest Iowa within fairly weak deep-layer shear and up to 2000 J/kg MUCAPE. Storms produced locally damaging wind as cores collapsed, and training of storms resulted in locally heavy rainfall of 2 to 3 inches around the Iowa Great Lakes in the matter of an hour or two.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs from 10 to 12 inches in diameter fell onto power lines from the electric pole to garage.  Winds were accompanied by hail up to dime size and torrential rainfall. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon on September 21st, a supercell thunderstorm briefly passed through northeastern Allamakee County. As the storm moved through it produced small hail and some minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported nickel sized hail 2 miles east of New Albin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon on September 21st, a supercell thunderstorm briefly passed through northeastern Allamakee County. As the storm moved through it produced small hail and some minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported tree branches down on State Highway 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along a boundary draped across northern Iowa on the afternoon of September 19th with additional storms through the evening on a cold front that moved across the area later in the evening. With over 2000 J/kg of available MLCAPE, steep lapse rates, and modestly curved hodographs that supported rotating updrafts, hail was reported in several of the storms that developed. Storms later into the evening produced wind damage in parts of southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delayed report. Two maple trees blown down. Damage to the top of an oak tree. Power lines damaged due to fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More isolated afternoon and early evening storms erupted just north of a frontal boundary across northeast Nebraska and western Iowa, which produced up to quarter size hail and damaging wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph. Storms became more widespread during the evening given a mid-level impulse and increase in the low-level jet across the frontal boundary. The rain regenerated in many of the same areas as the previous night's excessive rainfall and brought two-day totals to as high as 8 to 16 inches setting several records. Widespread flash flooding developed during the evening and overnight hours with catastrophic damage in Rock Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the effective frontal boundary forced well southward by overnight and early morning convection, only isolated elevated thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and evening hours.  The strongest cells produced hail up to quarter size with marginal elevated instability and effective shear around 30 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated, diurnally driven thunderstorms developed in the afternoon within an axis of instability that stretched into southwest Iowa. A few reports of hail between nickel to half dollar sized were reported in Adams county with other storms remaining weaker.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 to 1.25 inch hail occurring between 2:32 to 2:45 pm near Corning on Carl Rd and 183rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure locked in very warm and extremely humid conditions. Dew points in the upper 70s and lower 80s combined with temperatures in the upper 80s to lower 90s to produce daily heat index values from 100 to 120 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon and early evening heat index values near Maurice reached as high as 108 degrees on August 25 and 111 degrees on August 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved into Iowa from South Dakota and Nebraska. Convection developed in ahead of, and with the shortwave axis, enhancing later in the evening with the development of the low level jet. ECAPE exceeded 1000 J/kg with effective shear of 30-40+ kts to aid in severe storm development. Additionally, directional shear in the low levels supported rotating updrafts supportive of hail development. Storms across the area produces penny to quarter sized hail with one report up to the size of golf balls.

Harvest equipment ignited a fire a mile to the west-northwest of Henderson. The fire was ignited near the intersection of Brothers Avenue and 380th Street. The fire burned 20 acres and destroyed farm equipment. Pottawattamie County Emergency Management reported on injury associated with this fire. Additional small fires were reported across Pottawattamie County this day as well, though they all burned under 20 acres and did not cause any damage to homes or casualties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harvest equipment ignited a fire a mile to the west-northwest of Henderson. The fire was ignited near the intersection of Brothers Avenue and 380th Street. The fire burned 20 acres and destroyed farm equipment. Pottawattamie County Emergency Management reported on injury associated with this fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most locations during September and October across northwest Iowa recorded a top-ten driest two-month period, which extended general dry weather since July and spurred re-emergence of severe drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought developed with precipitation from 4 to 5 inches below normal since August 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave passed across the area, bringing widespread rain with over two inches falling in much of southeast Iowa. By afternoon weak instability developed, and when combined with robust available shear, was enough to produce isolated hail and damaging wind gusts. Storms organized into a QLCS in the evening and while hodographs were supportive of rotating updrafts and the potential for tornadoes, storms remained just elevated and prevented any further development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personal weather station recorded a 60 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas ahead of a weak frontal boundary exhibited pooling of moisture with dew point readings into the upper 70s to lower 80s. As temperatures reached the lower to mid 90s, dangerous heat conditions developed during the afternoon hours with heat index values from 100 to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heat index values peaked very briefly at 105 degrees at a personal weather station 5 miles south-southwest of Ellsworth and 101 degrees at Inwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of minimal precipitation across northeast Iowa from the beginning of September and throughout much of October resulted in rainfall deficits of 4 to 7 inches below normal. As a result, severe drought conditions developed during the middle of October in northeast Iowa. Severe drought conditions persisted through the end of the month until a substantial rainfall event brought 0.5 to 2 inches of rainfall across northeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Throughout the entirety of September and through October 29th, only 0.5 to 1 inches of precipitation fell in Fayette County. This 5 to 7 inch deficit for this period resulted in the development of severe drought by mid-October. Severe drought conditions ended in the county by October 31st when roughly 1 to 2 inches of rainfall fell across the county in the final week of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a relatively mild summer, a period of excessive heat impacted much of the region prompting air temperatures to reach well above 90 degrees during the afternoon on August 26th. This combined with dewpoints as high as 80 degrees in some locations allowed for heat indices to climb above 110 degrees on August 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anomalously warm air temperatures and high humidity allowed the heat index to reach between 110 to 118 degrees on August 26th across Mitchell County. Heat index values reached 118 degrees at a cooperative observation site in St. Ansgar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving shortwave passed across the area in the very early morning hours of October 4th. Despite modest available moisture fields, strong forcing and the deepening low were enough for convection to develop over central to eastern Iowa. Storms produced large amounts of pea sized hail in some locations with other areas reporting up to golf ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved into Iowa from South Dakota and Nebraska. Convection developed in ahead of, and with the shortwave axis, enhancing later in the evening with the development of the low level jet. ECAPE exceeded 1000 J/kg with effective shear of 30-40+ kts to aid in severe storm development. Additionally, directional shear in the low levels supported rotating updrafts supportive of hail development. Storms across the area produces penny to quarter sized hail with one report up to the size of golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across the Iowa Great Lakes during the mid-afternoon hours of August 29th.  Along the convergence boundary on the leading edge of the developing storms, a quick spin up developed and dissipated over Big Spirit Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick spin-up developed along an advancing line of thunderstorms across Big Spirit Lake. The waterspout tracked northeast for around one minute for a little less than a mile before dissipating. The entire life cycle of the tornado was captured on video. No damage occurred from which a rating could be effectively determined.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cooler air wrapped into a low pressure system, which developed along a slow moving frontal boundary across Iowa and Minnesota early on October 31. Rain changed to snowfall, which accumulated mainly at locations with slightly higher elevation. With winds gusting to 25 to 35 mph, there were brief periods of visibility less than a mile with slick conditions resulting in numerous traffic accidents on secondary roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light to occasionally moderate snowfall totaled 1.7 inches at Sibley. Northwest winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph during snowfall and resulted in reduced visibility as low as 1 mile at times of greater snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed late in the day on Aug 27th in northeast Missouri and spread into southeast Iowa and far western Illinois into the evening. Large hail up to golf ball size fell for nearly one half hour straight in Rutledge, MO.||A pronounced downburst (powerful winds that descend from a thunderstorm and spread out quickly once they hit the ground) caused widespread damage in Burlington, IA, knocking down trees, limbs, and powerlines. Five homes had holes in the roofs due to falling limbs. A commercial storage building had a partial roof collapse and another large tall outbuilding had partial roofing peeled back, consistent with wind estimates of at least 80 mph. ||Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were observed in parts of northeast Missouri and southeast Iowa. ||Oppressive heat and humidity that began on the 26th and persisted into the 27th helped spawn these storms. Both Dubuque (tied) and Moline (exceeded) daily record highs on the 27th, with Moline reaching 98�� and Dubuque 94��.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anomalously warm air temperatures and high humidity allowed the heat index to reach between 108 and 120 degrees across Floyd County on August 26th. The heat index reached 117 degrees at the automated weather observation station at Charles City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: PLEASE NOTE, for hail, wind, heavy rain and flooding, please see separate Storm Data entry for May 20-22. ||Numerous tornadoes occurred on this day with numerous injuries and four fatalities at Greenfield in Adair County.||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall, damaging winds, hail and destructive tornadoes to the state. These thunderstorms were the result of a low pressure system which lifted north into the area on the night of May 20th, 2024, producing slow moving storms that dropped 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding over portions of northern and central Iowa through the morning of May 21st, 2024.||Following the heavy rainfall through the morning, skies cleared out ahead of an approaching cold front. By the afternoon hours of May 21st, 2024, this cold front produced a line of strong thunderstorms which produced multiple strong tornadoes throughout the state, including one which led to significant damage in the town of Greenfield, IA, among other locations. This line of storms also produced damaging winds, leading to widespread tree damage and power outages throughout the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management recorded a path of damage from just east of Greenfield to just shy of the Adair/Madison County line north of 140th Street.  The initial portion of this tornado was observed via the Doppler on Wheels (DOW) and was extended farther southwest accordingly.  This tornado  impacted farmsteads with damage to outbuildings and tree groves.  Toward the end of the path, a large industrial wind mill was blown over southeast of the intersection of 150th street and Walnut Avenue.   EF2 damage was then done with complete destruction of an outbuilding along York Avenue along with shredding of trees.  The tornado then quickly weakened and dissipated as it approached the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid-afternoon into the early evening on December 4, a powerful cold front stretching from a weather system in the northern Great Lakes region swept through eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. This front ushered in much colder air along with very strong gradient winds. None of the wind gusts were associated with any thunderstorms. Several wind gusts over 58 mph were recorded across the region, and several semi trailers were blown over in northeast Iowa near Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Cedar Rapids Airport automated weather equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the daytime on December 14th, widespread freezing rain occurred across eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central and northwest Illinois.|Some of the more notable impacts were; Numerous slick roads in and around the Cedar Rapids, IA metro area where over a half inch of flat ice was observed (CID Airport total of 0.68). Iowa City, IA saw over a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Multiple jackknifed trucks on I-80 as reported through social media. Numerous branches down across the area due to icing. These along with ice on wires caused power outages, with some counties in southeast Iowa temporarily having 30% of customers without power.|McDonough County in west central Illinois, including the Macomb area, where icing over 0.25 resulted in several large branches down due to ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and DOT reported widespread slick conditions with a 0.1 to 0.3 of an inch of ice accumulation on the ground and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-air analysis at 12 UTC on the 19th showed troughing over the eastern CONUS and ridging over the western CONUS. In the northern Great Plains, a shortwave trough embedded in northwesterly flow aloft dove southeast over the course of the day. At the surface, a strong low-pressure system was analyzed in the Dakotas with a warm front extending into the lower-Missouri River Valley, and a cold front extending west into the northern High Plains. This storm system dove southeast into the upper-Mississippi River Valley as the cold front surged south across the OAX forecast area. Very strong flow of 70 knots at the top of the boundary layer, aided by subsidence immediately behind the cold front brought very strong winds gusting 60 to 65 miles per hour during the early and mid afternoon hours. As a result of these strong winds, isolated power outages, downed trees, and overturned semis were all reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of high winds were observed during the afternoon hours. The strongest observed wind gust in the county was 59 miles per hour, observed by a personal weather station at 1:36 pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on December 4th, an arctic cold front progressed through the region bringing strong winds along with it. Frequent wind gusts of 40-50 mph in Clayton County were responsible for tipping over a semi-trailer near St. Olaf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a semi trailer was blown over near St. Olaf when encountering strong winds. A nearby automated weather observation station at Prairie Du Chien, Wisconsin reported wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph during the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on December 4th, an arctic cold front progressed through the region bringing strong winds along with it. Frequent wind gusts of 40-50 mph in Clayton County were responsible for tipping over a semi-trailer near St. Olaf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 40 mph persisted across portions of Chickasaw County during the late morning through early evening hours on December 4th. Additionally, a 58 mph peak non-thunderstorm wind gust was observed at an Iowa Department of Transportation weather observation station in New Hampton during this period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet quickly transitioned over to freezing rain across portions of northeast Iowa during the afternoon of December 14th. This resulted in ice accumulations from 0.1 to 0.3 across Fayette and Clayton counties prior to the storm exiting the region later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet began across Clayton County during the early morning hours, by the afternoon numerous locations across the county had reported ice amounts ranging from 0.15 to 0.3. The highest of these was in Guttenberg, IA which reported 0.3 of ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet quickly transitioned over to freezing rain across portions of northeast Iowa during the afternoon of December 14th. This resulted in ice accumulations from 0.1 to 0.3 across Fayette and Clayton counties prior to the storm exiting the region later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced freezing rain and sleet across much of Fayette County resulting in 0.13 of ice accumulation in Fayette, IA. Radar estimates were anywhere from 0.1 to 0.2 of ice accumulation across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust shortwave and associated cold front swept across the area on December 4th. The strong cold air advection and subsidence helped to pull stronger winds to the surface, enhancing already strong winds due to the tight pressure gradient across the area. Winds gusted 40-55+ mph throughout the afternoon and evening hours across the area. A few stronger gusts from 58 to 63 mph also occurred in parts of northern and central Iowa.

Dense fog blanketed the area on the the morning of December 28th with a dense fog advisory in effect for visibility under a quarter mile. Unfortunately a car accident that morning resulted in three fatalities and three injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area observations recorded visibility under one quarter mile overnight and through the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid-afternoon into the early evening on December 4, a powerful cold front stretching from a weather system in the northern Great Lakes region swept through eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. This front ushered in much colder air along with very strong gradient winds. None of the wind gusts were associated with any thunderstorms. Several wind gusts over 58 mph were recorded across the region, and several semi trailers were blown over in northeast Iowa near Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four semi trailers were reported blown over due to strong gradient winds, not associated with thunderstorms. These semis were on Highway 151 and Highway 61. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the daytime on December 14th, widespread freezing rain occurred across eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central and northwest Illinois.|Some of the more notable impacts were; Numerous slick roads in and around the Cedar Rapids, IA metro area where over a half inch of flat ice was observed (CID Airport total of 0.68). Iowa City, IA saw over a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Multiple jackknifed trucks on I-80 as reported through social media. Numerous branches down across the area due to icing. These along with ice on wires caused power outages, with some counties in southeast Iowa temporarily having 30% of customers without power.|McDonough County in west central Illinois, including the Macomb area, where icing over 0.25 resulted in several large branches down due to ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and DOT reported widespread slick conditions with a 0.1 to 0.3 of an inch of ice accumulation on the ground and elevated surfaces.

Winds were sustained at times from 30 to 39 mph with gusts as high as 50 to 55 mph, strongest toward the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were from 30 to 37 mph at times near Rock Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-air analysis at 12 UTC on the 19th showed troughing over the eastern CONUS and ridging over the western CONUS. In the northern Great Plains, a shortwave trough embedded in northwesterly flow aloft dove southeast over the course of the day. At the surface, a strong low-pressure system was analyzed in the Dakotas with a warm front extending into the lower-Missouri River Valley, and a cold front extending west into the northern High Plains. This storm system dove southeast into the upper-Mississippi River Valley as the cold front surged south across the OAX forecast area. Very strong flow of 70 knots at the top of the boundary layer, aided by subsidence immediately behind the cold front brought very strong winds gusting 60 to 65 miles per hour during the early and mid afternoon hours. As a result of these strong winds, isolated power outages, downed trees, and overturned semis were all reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though no direct measurements were made in the county of severe level wind gusts, observations from surrounding counties support the high likelihood of wind gusts up to 58 mph occurring the afternoon of the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave deepened across the Rockies and into the Midwest with the surface low tracking across Kansas and into northern Missouri. Precipitation, mainly liquid rain, lifted north into the area on the evening of December 13th, quickly changing over to freezing rain around and shortly after midnight in central to southeast Iowa. Ice continued to accumulate through about noon when conditions began to warm enough to a transition back to liquid rain. Ice amounts were highest in eastern portions of Iowa where a quarter inch or more was reported with a lighter glaze towards central Iowa. This resulted in a number of accidents on area roads as conditions quickly became slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tree damage.

An area of low pressure intensified lifting northward across Minnesota into southwestern Ontario and resulted in very strong winds across northwest Iowa which gusted as high as to 50 to 55 mph. Some limited moisture wrapped back southward with snowfall up to a few tenths of an inch at most.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 49 mph occurred at Sergeant Bluff and 46 mph at Moville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed the night of November 14th and continued through the morning of November 15th. Visibility was under one quarter mile across much of Iowa during this time with a Dense Fog Advisory in effect. This resulted in a car crash with one fatality and two injuries in northern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area observations recorded visibility under one quarter mile overnight and through the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified lifting northward across Minnesota into southwestern Ontario and resulted in very strong winds across northwest Iowa which gusted as high as to 50 to 55 mph. Some limited moisture wrapped back southward with snowfall up to a few tenths of an inch at most.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 30 to 39 mph were common 5 miles south of Ellsworth, with a few gusts up to around 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation from 150 to 250 percent of normal relaxed drought conditions over much of northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought at the start of November improved to moderate (D1) status at mid-month with 2 to 3 inches of rainfall during the first half of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels from the historic June flooding remained above flood stage for some locations into July, although continued a declining trend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from June on the Big Sioux River near Brandon, with declining river levels. The river fell below flood stage on the morning of July 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet surging into the region, paired with increasing warm air and moisture advection into the morning hours of May 24th, led to the development of a line of showers and storms (QLCS) in Nebraska, which then swept across Iowa. MUCAPE values around 2000J/kg ahead of this line allowed for these storms to become severe within the QLCS as it rapidly tracked eastward across Iowa through the morning. Severe hail occurred with these storms, with values generally between 1-1.75 inches, though hail up to 2.5-2.75 inches was reported in Wayne county. Outside of the hail, severe winds were the primary hazard, with values reported generally between 58-75 mph. Widespread damage was reported due to the winds, with the majority of this damage being snapped trees/branches and power poles, some falling onto structures, as well as blown over semis. Outside of the hail and winds, a handful of tornadoes also occurred (please refer to the separate entry for those details).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines and trees down, along with flipped campers. Time of occurrence estimated with radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: PLEASE NOTE, for hail, wind, heavy rain and flooding, please see separate Storm Data entry for May 20-22. ||Numerous tornadoes occurred on this day with numerous injuries and four fatalities at Greenfield in Adair County.||Multiple rounds of thunderstorms brought torrential rainfall, damaging winds, hail and destructive tornadoes to the state. These thunderstorms were the result of a low pressure system which lifted north into the area on the night of May 20th, 2024, producing slow moving storms that dropped 2 to 4 inches of rainfall. This resulted in flash flooding over portions of northern and central Iowa through the morning of May 21st, 2024.||Following the heavy rainfall through the morning, skies cleared out ahead of an approaching cold front. By the afternoon hours of May 21st, 2024, this cold front produced a line of strong thunderstorms which produced multiple strong tornadoes throughout the state, including one which led to significant damage in the town of Greenfield, IA, among other locations. This line of storms also produced damaging winds, leading to widespread tree damage and power outages throughout the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports via social media of tree damage associated with this tornado.  It also produced a TDS on radar as it moved to the northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) swept across Iowa late on May 6th and into the early morning hours of May 7th. The line was strongest in southern Iowa where the environment was more supportive of severe weather. Damaging winds and a few embedded tornadoes occurred as the line swept across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous posts on social media regarding damage on the east side of Prairie City.  A very brief tornado occurred here with damage mostly to trees although a few garages sustained minor damage.  The tornado lifted shortly after leaving town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area bringing hail and damaging wind gusts to central Iowa. The first round moved across the area around midday as remnants of an MCS out of Kansas and Nebraska moved into the area. By afternoon a front lifted across the area and afternoon heating allowed for thunderstorm redevelopment, becoming severe with MLCAPE exceeding 2000 J/kg and increasing deep layer shear. Later in the evening a line of thunderstorms moved out of Nebraska and into Iowa, sustained by a strong low-level jet. This bowing line of storms was mostly sub severe, but did result in thunderstorm wind damage late in eastern portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of quarter sized hail near the intersection of Highway 92 and Henry A Wallace Road. Time estimated with radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across Worth County on the evening of August 5th producing 3 tornadoes.  The tornadoes remained in rural cropland and were all rated as EFUs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado observed by spotters with track found in high resolution satellite imagery. The tornado remained in rural cropland with no impacts to damage indicators, hence the EFU rating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado observed by spotters with track found in high resolution satellite imagery. This tornado had a looping track with the actual path length of nearly 1.6 miles instead of the 0.84 straight line distance provided by Storm Data. The tornado remained in rural cropland with no impacts to damage indicators, hence the EFU rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving into western Iowa by the evening hours, paired with a warm and moist environment ahead of it into Iowa resulted in the development of showers and storms. Specific environmental parameters that were favorable for storms to develop and grow upscale along the cold front included wind shear values around 30-35 knots, along with 1500-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE and 1000+ J/kg of DCAPE.  Though severe winds were the most probable hazard, reports received were almost none. One report of thunderstorm wind damage came from Humboldt county, where several large tree branches broke off trees in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches down at this location, with additional damage up to 2 miles north of this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After morning convection across much of Iowa, additional convection occurred during the late afternoon to evening, with the majority of activity over southern Iowa. Conditions were suitable for storm development, which included 35-45 knots of 0-6 km bulk shear and 2000-3000+ J/kg of MLCAPE, with a shortwave pushing across the area. Most notable was the near severe hail that occurred in Poweshiek county from one rather strong cell, with reported hail at 0.75 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated to be the size of a grape, or 0.75 inches.
